data.title: GE Vernova: A Textbook Bear Trap Post Q3 2025 Earnings
data.description:
In my earnings preview on GE Vernova Inc. (NYSE:GEV), I wrote the following as the company prepared to release its Q3 2025 results:

In fact, I believe the company would be better off by divesting this segment [Wind], despite the fact that management expects EBITDA breakeven in this segment sometime in H2 2025.

Now, you understand why I wrote that.

I'll be direct: I didn't expect such a dramatic selloff post earnings. In fact, not too many people did, which is the reason shares are up by mid-single digits from the pre-Q3 earnings release at the time of writing this article. Call me crazy, but I think what we saw yesterday was a classical bear trap.

Let's address the downside first.

Management cut FY25 wind revenue to down high single digits organically (from down mid-single digits) due to continued softness in onshore equipment orders through the year. Tariffs are weighing on this segment, now expected in the lower end of the $300–$400M range. Tariffs were also among the reasons why the company missed earnings by 22 cents, as they had a negative impact on onshore margin expansion.

The current FY25 EBITDA expectation for the wind segment is approximately a loss of $400M. Not the best news, if one ignores the other two segments, which (surprise, surprise) are the meat of the bone when it comes to growth. And, considering that investors are paying today 80x next year's earnings, growth is the main chapter in the narrative surrounding this stock. In my experience as a market practitioner, I believe the narrative plays a fundamental role in the midst of a broader bull run, especially with the data center buildout tailwind affecting this company. After Q3 2025 earnings, I strongly believe GE Vernova's growth story remains intact.

Q3 2025 Earnings Overview

I will discuss the downside (i.e., the wind segment) in detail in the next section. Here, I focus on the two segments that are driving the double-digit growth of the company: wind and electrification.

Starting with the latter, electrification orders came in at $5.1B, equivalent to 2x revenue of this segment in Q3, and growing more than 2x YOY. The equipment backlog is now sitting at $26B (an increase of $8B vs 3Q24). Revenue was up by 32% and EBITDA doubled, with a margin improvement of 500 bps. Full-year organic revenue growth is expected to come in at 25%, which is an increase from the previous guidance of 20%. On hyperscaler demand, here are the remarks of CEO Scott Strazik:

Hyperscalers increasingly are turning to us for their electrification needs with $400 million of orders in 3Q alone. So far this year, we've booked roughly $900 million in electrification orders with hyperscalers compared to $600 million in all of '24.

Looking at the power segment, the company signed 12 GW worth of new contracts in Q3 (after 9 GW in Q2). The most important product line, gas turbines, has seen its backlog increase to 33 GW from 29GW at the end of Q2. Overall, GE Vernova's total commitments add up to 62 GW, with management now upgrading the guidance for year-end (previously, the guidance was broad, at +60 GW):

We now expect to approach 70 gigawatts of contractual gas power commitments by the end of '25 with significant momentum into '26.

The statement above from the CEO is as bullish as one can get on this stock.

Looking at the overall results, Q3 orders came in at $14.6B, representing a decent 55% YOY growth, with a backlog now sitting at $135B. Organic revenue increased by 10% YOY as the company continues to see strength in electrification orders from hyperscalers, as I already noted above. On the bottom line, adjusted EBITDA margins expanded by 600 bps YOY. The guidance for adj. EBITDA margins in FY2025 is now sitting at 8–9%, with free cash flows expected to land in the $3.0–$3.5B range.

Overall, I see the growth engines intact. Let's move on to dessert now.

The Wind Drag

Let's first clarify some key items in regard to guidance for FY25. Management downgraded wind’s FY25 revenue outlook, now expected to be "down high single digits" organically from the prior expectation of "down mid-single digits." Let's take a closer look at the 10-Q to understand the relevance of the wind segment.

As seen above, Wind is (by a mere $46M) the second largest segment for the top line of the company. Now, the big question is: is this segment relevant for investors? After all, we are all paying a premium on the share price, and I am here to say that the reason we do is one word: growth. As noted above, the growth engine (or should I say, engines) of the company is not the wind segment. This segment has been declining steadily since the end of last year.

In the latest update, management stated the main driver behind this deterioration:

Customers still face permitting delays and tariff uncertainty that will likely weigh on our '26 onshore revenue.

I believe the last part of the sentence above didn't sit well with investors, which was another reason for the exaggerated selloff post-earnings.

Looking below at the YOY revenue change of the other two segments, it is clear which segments are relevant for investors.

Furthermore, the company-level guidance remained intact.

In fact, the company raised FY25 guidance in electrification (take a look again at the two charts above, and you will notice an impressive 34.9% YOY growth in Q3 2025), now expecting organic growth to be 25% YOY from the previous estimate of 20%. Furthermore, EBITDA margin for this segment was guided at 14–15%, effectively raising the lower end of the prior guidance of 13%-15%.

Let me be clear: if I had seen the slightest signs of weakness in any of these two segments, the article that you are reading now would have had a strong sell rating, along with a disclosure of a short position in the form OTM puts. I am completely detached from the stocks that I cover, and I have no issues reverting my longs to shorts when the narrative cracks. So far, GE Vernova's story is far from that.

Can The Shares Trade Back in the Low-$500s?

Yes. However, I am not a short-term trader, so this volatility doesn't make me sweat as I'm looking in the long term.

As seen in the chart below, the consolidation around the high $500s was broken to the downside, which is often a signal for the bears to step in and short a stock.

In my experience as a market practitioner, I believe the post-earnings selloff could be what is often called a bear trap, as today the share price is higher than the close prior to Q3 earnings.

Is this making me sweat? No. In fact, I believe it may be a good moment to lower my cost basis on this stock.

On The Valuation "Thing"

Quite often, I find articles with a headline similar to the one below:

Look, as an aeronautical engineer working in the commercial industry for almost a decade now, I love models, abstract formulas, and unnecessarily complex mathematical equations more than I want to recognize. That said, I am here to ask (particularly to the value guys) a very simple question: what's the formula, baby?

I can tell you that, after researching quantitative methods for well over a decade, I came to the conclusion that there isn't such a thing. Valuation analysis by itself does not offer any upside. If it did, all value investors out there would be in the triple-digit return per year, and I can tell from knowing many value guys, that ain't the case, most of the time.

Therefore, I am not content with the argument that high multiples equal downside ahead. At the time of writing this article, GE Vernova is trading at a forward P/E multiple of 80. That's beyond the multiple of any of the Magnificent 7 (except, of course, Tesla). However, just take a look at the chart below, showing YTD returns, and you will quickly see who is in the top position.

Expensive stocks outperform when the narrative remains intact. In the case of GE Vernova, that story is mainly revolving around the AI datacenter buildout around the world, particularly in the U.S. As long as hyperscalers are maintaining their CapEx plans and keep announcing GW-scale data center projects, the need for gas turbines and grid equipment will remain elevated. Furthermore, by looking at the change in orders below, I have strong reasons to believe the two main growth engines are intact.

Looking Ahead

Even though price action doesn't look particularly attractive in the near term, I believe there is further upside ahead, particularly after seeing the strong growth in orders (55% YOY) in the last quarter.

On the Prolec GE acquisition, I don't think it plays a fundamental role in the bull case, even though management is expecting it to be immediately accretive to EBITDA once completed in mid-2026. To fund the purchase of the remaining 50% of Prolec GE JV from Xignux, worth $5.275 B, the company will be using 50% cash and 50% debt. I believe this is prudent, and I am really happy to not see a dilution round, particularly now that shares are performing better than the Mag 7.

Overall, I reiterate my bull case on GE Vernova, although I warn that further volatility is not completely off the table.

data.title: Twilio Q3 Preview: 3 Catalysts I Am Looking Out For
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Twilio Inc. (NYSE:TWLO) in August, I reiterated my “buy” rating on the stock, as I believed that the post-Q2 FY25 earnings selloff had created a unique buying opportunity. The way I saw it, Twilio had posted impressive Q2 earnings, accelerating their revenue growth for four straight quarters, while holding on to its promise of delivering break-even for its Segment business and raising its full-year guidance.

The stock has since outperformed the S&P 500, while I have recently added the company to my brand new portfolio that I started last month, where I take a fundamental approach to investing in some of the enablers and beneficiaries of the AI boom that are trading at a discount to my price targets.

Twilio is slated to report its Q3 FY25 earnings on 10/30, where management is projecting a sequential deceleration in revenue growth and non-GAAP operating margin. In this post, I will outline the three things that I believe need to happen in order to renew investor confidence in the stock.

1. Revenue Needs To Grow 13% YoY At Least

In the previous quarter, Twilio grew its revenues by 13% YoY to $1.228B, marking four consecutive quarters of revenue acceleration, while simultaneously beating management guidance that called for a 9-10% YoY revenue growth for the quarter.

For Q3 FY25, management has guided for revenues of $1.25B, which would represent a sequential deceleration in the growth rate to 10.5% YoY. Meanwhile, consensus estimates are also at par with management’s guidance. What will reignite investor confidence in the stock is if Twilio can beat its revenue guidance by at least 250 basis points, growing 13% YoY. This would keep the company’s revenue acceleration trend intact for five quarters in a row, as can be seen below.

Over the past four quarters, Twilio has beat its revenue guidance by an average of 280 basis points every quarter. This is higher than the 250 basis point beat that is required to keep the company’s revenue acceleration trend intact.

What is also optimistic is that over the last three months, Twilio has been receiving upward revisions to its forward revenue estimates, which now point to the company growing in the high single digits after FY25. This is still conservative in my opinion, given that management has hinted at the possibility of Twilio growing in double digits from FY25 onwards in their Investor Presentation.

The upward revisions in forward revenue estimates are driven by ratings upgrades from Rosenblatt, Wells Fargo, and Piper Sandler. Catharine Trebnick from Rosenblatt believes that the company sits at a key inflection point where years of investment in market leadership are set to translate into a durable model of profitable growth and significant free cash flow generation, while setting a price target of $140 on Twilio’s stock.

"The combination of a massive cross-selling opportunity, a dominant position as the communications backbone for the AI ecosystem, and a new commitment to substantial shareholder returns creates a compelling risk/reward profile at current levels," said Trebnick and her team.

Meanwhile, Wells Fargo also initiated coverage of Twilio with a price target of $130 per share, as they believe the company is a well-positioned infrastructure provider for the voice AI applications.

"We are noticing stronger voice AI momentum (checks/startups noting increasing wins), and in 2Q25 TWLO noted voice revenue growth improved back to above double digits," said Wells Fargo analysts Ryan MacWilliams and Chris Brazeau in an investor note. "Startups are starting with Twilio by utilizing TWLO's APIs and SDKs to build their own AI agents on top of TWLO's rails. OpenAI's Realtime API (went GA at the end of August) powers complex voice AI conversations and is often utilized in conjunction with TWLO by developers."

The way I see it, one of the factors that has allowed Twilio to accelerate its revenue for four straight quarters so far has been the momentum in its large deals. In the previous quarter, large deals closed over $500,000 grew 57% YoY, with double-digit growth in multi-product customers, leading to their dollar-based net expansion rate growing sequentially higher every single quarter over the last five quarters.

Moving forward, I will be paying attention to the momentum in large deals as well as the dollar-based expansion rate, as the company tries to capture its $158B TAM, consisting of $119B from its Communications and Data and $39B from its emerging CXaaS (Customer Experience as a Service) market, with products such as Verify Fraud Guard, Unified Profiles & Agent Copilot, AI-Powered Predictions, and Predictive Audience driving greater customer value and higher ROI.

2. Non-GAAP Operating Margin Needs To Stay Anchored At 18%

The second area that Twilio needs to outperform in Q3 is profitability.

In the previous quarter, the non-GAAP operating margin for Twilio came in at $221M with a margin of 18%, flat sequentially but up on a year-over-year basis, with its Segment business reporting a non-GAAP operating margin of 8%, as opposed to -2% in the previous quarter.

However, what dampened investor sentiment in the previous quarter was management’s guidance for non-GAAP operating margin for Q3, where they are expected to see a contraction of 120 basis points sequentially to 16.8%.

In order for the stock to regain its momentum, I believe that Twilio would have to deliver a non-GAAP operating margin of at least 18%, which would require a 120 basis point beat. This would keep its margin trend anchored for three quarters in a row.

What is optimistic is that the company has been receiving upward revisions from consensus to its forward earnings per share estimates over the last month, which could be a prelude to a possible beat in Q3 earnings growth.

3. Full Year FY25 Guidance Needs To Be Revised Higher

Finally, when it comes to full-year guidance, it would help revive investor confidence if management raised their revenue and earnings projections higher than what was projected in the previous quarter.

In Q2 FY25, the management projected full-year revenues to grow 10-11%, with a non-GAAP operating margin of close to 17.5%. Plus, the management also noted that there was no change in their 2027 financial targets that they laid out during their Investor Presentation from earlier this year, where they are targeting a non-GAAP operating margin of 21-22% by 2027 while generating a cumulative $3B in free cash flow during this period of time.

Like I said earlier, consensus estimates are likely underestimating the company’s forward revenue growth rates beyond FY25, which leaves plenty of room for the stock to be rerated higher.

However, in order for that to happen, Twilio needs to demonstrate not only a Q3 revenue and earnings beat in the magnitude as I discussed earlier but also raise forward guidance. This is especially the case now as we see a recession in the software sector from potential fears of AI disruption. This has led to many companies, including Twilio, seeing a contraction in their forward multiples, despite no changes to earnings growth estimates.

Revisiting My Valuation

In one of my earlier posts this month, I discussed that I started a brand new portfolio called The Pragmatic Optimist portfolio at the end of September, where I would take a fundamental approach to investing in some of the enablers and beneficiaries of the AI boom that are trading at a discount to our price targets.

So far, I have deployed close to 50% of the $30,000 in initial planned capital, and it is currently outperforming the S&P 500. In the post, I discussed my rationale for investing in ServiceNow, Zscaler, Toast, Duolingo, and The Trade Desk, which make up over 10% of my portfolio.

Over the last month, I have also initiated a position in Twilio for The Pragmatic Optimist portfolio, allocating a little over 1.0%, with plans to increase my position size to at least 3% of the total portfolio.

At the moment, if I take consensus estimates into account, Twilio should be growing its revenues by 10.75% YoY in FY25, followed by growth in the high single digits in the following years, translating to a projected $5.78B by FY27. As I wrote earlier, I believe this is easily achievable given Twilio’s robust product innovation roadmap in the Communications, Data, and AI market, with the opportunity to drive deeper adoption of its solutions via cross-sell, ISVs, and its international markets.

Meanwhile, from a profitability standpoint, Twilio is expected to deliver 21-22% in non-GAAP operating margin by FY27, which will translate to $1.25B in non-GAAP operating income, or an equivalent of $1.03B in present value, when discounted at 8%.

The stock is currently trading at a forward PE of 24, which is more or less at par with the sector median. Assuming that the multiple stays anchored at current levels over the next two years, we arrive at a price target of $160 per share, representing an upside of 47% from its current levels.

Risks And Conclusions

The biggest risks remain that the company will see a sequential contraction in revenue growth and non-GAAP operating margin in Q3, followed by lackluster guidance for the full year in its earnings next week.

However, I believe that the risk-reward is attractive at these levels. As a result, I will not be selling my shares across portfolios and may even increase my position size in my new The Pragmatic Optimist portfolio to 2.0% before earnings next week.

In the meantime, I will update my price target to $160 per share, representing an upside of 47% from its current levels.

data.title: Astera Labs: A Strong AI Infrastructure Play, But Priced For Perfection
data.description:
Introduction:

Astera Labs, Inc. (NASDAQ:ALAB) is one of the most interesting companies tied to the AI infrastructure space. The firm's products play a critical role in helping move data faster and more efficiently across servers inside modern data centers, which is critical for training and inference of generative AI models.

With strong demand for the company's products, including the Aries, Scorpio, Taurus, and Leo lines, I believe that Astera is positioned well to capitalize on the buildout of large rack-scale systems over the coming years.

However, despite the company's impressive growth trajectory and a number of potential future catalysts, I believe that the current valuation already reflects very optimistic assumptions from market participants. Despite the recent sell-off, I believe that new investors should wait for an even better entry point, and I am assigning shares of Astera Labs a HOLD rating.

Business Description & Key Considerations:

Astera Labs sells products that help move data between chips, servers, and storage systems inside data centers. The company's primary value proposition is centered around removing bottlenecks inside these data centers by providing high-speed connectivity solutions, and the Intelligent Connectivity Platform combines the firm's semiconductor hardware with the COSMOS software.

This platform tracks performance metrics such as the temperature levels and error rates of Astera's products, which are essential as these components are often deployed across thousands of different servers and racks. While the company's connectivity platform does help to differentiate its offerings, Astera does compete in an incredibly crowded market with lots of well-capitalized players who are developing similar interconnect solutions.

Growth this past quarter primarily came from two product categories, which were the signal conditioning products and fabric switches. The former refers to chips like Astera's Aries Retimers, which boost high-speed electrical signals as they travel between systems inside a server.

A major development this past quarter was the continued ramp-up in production of the company's Scorpio P-Series fabric switches, which are designed to support Peripheral Component Interconnect Express (PCIe) 6.0, the newest generation of the popular data transfer standard, which has doubled data throughput compared to PCIe 5.0.

The Scorpio product series is designed for AI workloads, as these switches operate at the I/O layer and directly manage data transfers between CPUs, GPUs, and accelerators inside racks instead of across the entire network like legacy Ethernet switches.

The company is already seeing strong early indications of demand for its next generation of fabric switches, dubbed the X-Series. Astera is currently expecting to start shipping the X-Series switches by the end of this year, with high-volume production slated to begin sometime in 2026. So far, 10 AI companies and cloud infrastructure providers have shown interest in this product, which means the company could further solidify its positioning as the preeminent interconnect fabric provider for next-generation data center operators.

Astera's customers primarily utilize these fabric switches to connect various components within a single server or at the rack level, which is referred to as scale-up architecture. When a data center operator wants to make a more powerful server to run complex workloads, they will add more components like CPUs, GPUs, memory, and storage systems, which allows for more cost-effective performance improvements.

The disadvantage of scale-up architecture is that it has inherent scaling limitations since infrastructure providers can only add so many new components to a single server or rack before it becomes an impractical way to boost computing power.

After this point, operators must use scale-out connectivity to distribute workloads by connecting multiple server racks together to create what's referred to as a cluster.

To support this transition from scale-up to scale-out, Astera offers the Taurus Smart Cable Modules, which are chips built into Active Electrical Cables (AECs) that are designed to extend Ethernet connections within and between racks that are close to one another. These modules both manage and amplify signals that travel between servers and switches throughout a cluster, which helps to ensure that data isn't distorted or lost during the transmission process.

The firm also sells the Leo memory controllers, which allow servers that are equipped with Compute Express Link (CXL)-capable CPUs to share memory with GPUs inside the same cluster. This is incredibly important because it eliminates the need to copy data between processors, which improves performance and efficiency when running computationally intensive AI workloads.

I believe that a key catalyst for the Leo product in particular is the recently announced collaboration between SAP (SAP) and Microsoft (MSFT), as the two companies are working together to use CXL memory expansion to create large shared memory pools, which should improve the performance of the SAP HANA database product. I believe that this partnership validates the growing CXL hardware and software infrastructure that is being developed, which should eventually increase demand for products like the Leo memory connectivity controllers.

Astera is also expanding its partnership with NVIDIA (NVDA) to support NVLink Fusion, where the company will provide both retimers and CXL controllers that help maintain signal integrity inside NVIDIA's rack-scale systems. This collaboration enables hyperscalers and tier two cloud service providers (CSPs) to deploy NVIDIA's GB200 and GB300 rack-scale systems within existing PCIe infrastructures.

In addition to the NVIDIA partnership, Astera is now working with AIchip Technologies, which is a firm that helps AI startups design their own custom application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs). The two firms working together makes complete sense in my mind, as Astera's connectivity solutions can be directly embedded into the systems that AIchip helps its customers design.

This should also open up Astera's portfolio of products to an entirely new customer base, inclusive of emerging AI companies that may not yet generate meaningful revenue but often have significant amounts of venture-backed funding. By becoming a key connectivity partner early on in these firm's life cycles, Astera positions its solutions as an integral part of these companies' technology stacks, which should lead to recurring revenue as these products become outdated over time and need to be replaced.

Perhaps the most important long-term catalyst for Astera is the fact that, as rack-scale systems move to higher-speed interconnects like PCIe 6.0 and beyond, more retimers are required to maintain the integrity of signals moving between components inside server racks.

So, while transitioning between PCIe generations does lead to increased R&D costs for Astera as the company develops new compatible retimers, these costs are going to be offset by the pricing power gained through both improved performance and the necessity of equipping racks with the latest technology.

However, an important caveat here is that as competition intensifies and the industry eventually moves to PCIe 7.0, Astera could face pricing pressure or margin contraction as a result of other solutions being released or higher development costs associated with maintaining compliance with new standards. Therefore, sustaining or even improving current margins will depend on the firm's ability to scale efficiently and avoid falling behind other players in the industry.

Financials:

Astera has reported very strong results recently, with Q2 revenue coming in at $191.9 million (up 20% sequentially and 150% YoY). The strength in top-line sales is largely attributable to sustained demand for both the Aries and Taurus products, along with the newly introduced Scorpio P-Series switches.

It's also good to see that the firm has continued to diversify its revenue streams, which reduces risk for investors. This past quarter, no single customer made up more than 27% of total revenue (which is admittedly still relatively high), compared to Q2 FY24, where one customer made up 41% of sales.

The company is also improving its profitability profile, with gross margins and non-GAAP operating margins expanding by 110 bps and 550 bps QoQ, respectively. R&D expenses for the quarter rose by 66% YoY, which made up nearly 70% of total operating expenses incurred during the period. I view this as a positive, as R&D expenses have historically translated to massive gains for the company as they continue to innovate and create solutions that customers value.

Astera also generated a quarterly record of $133.4 million in FCF, which helped move the company's net cash position to over $1 billion. This was partially driven by the collection of accounts receivable (which is variable), along with improved bottom-line profitability.

Management is currently guiding for Q3 revenue to increase roughly 7.6% QoQ and 82.6% YoY at the midpoint of the range ($206.5 million). This growth is expected to be driven by continued adoption of the Aries retimers as well as the Taurus modules, along with continued contribution from the Scorpio fabric switches, as customers continue to deploy the P-Series products into GPU configurations.

Now let's turn to the valuation of the stock. Astera currently trades at over 100x non-GAAP forward earnings and a price-to-forward sales multiple in excess of 34x. While this may seem extreme, it is increasingly common for companies that are providing the hardware that is necessary to run data centers to trade at elevated valuation multiples due to high expectations for future growth.

However, given the fact that the company trades at nearly 48x 2028 earnings and over 16x 2028 sales, I believe that the company's current market cap is excessive and leaves too little margin for error. Therefore, I view investing at current share prices as too risky, even despite the sell-off over the past month.

Conclusion:

Astera Labs is executing extremely well, with robust revenue growth and impressive margins across the board. The company's portfolio of products is well aligned with the upcoming transition to PCIe 6.0, CXL, and UALink standards, and partnerships with key players in the AI space should allow sales and profits to continue growing for the foreseeable future.

However, while I do believe in Astera's ability to continue scaling and capturing demand from hyperscalers and smaller AI infrastructure providers, I also view the current stock price as too expensive.

Overall, I believe that Astera offers a compelling long-term growth story, and current investors may well benefit from short-term upside, but given the elevated multiples, I am assigning a HOLD rating, as I believe those looking to initiate a position should sit on the sidelines for a bit longer.

data.title: Avadel Settles With Jazz Pharmaceuticals, Gets Acquired By Alkermes
data.description:
Avadel Pharmaceuticals plc (NASDAQ:AVDL) and Jazz Pharmaceuticals plc (NASDAQ:JAZZ) announced a settlement today that resolves all outstanding disputes between the two companies, and then, Alkermes plc (ALKS) announced that it will acquire Avadel Pharmaceuticals for up to $20 per share.

This is a bit of a sad end for Avadel, as I do not believe the deal reflects the true value of the company, especially considering the clean legal road ahead after the settlement with Jazz. However, it also eliminates further execution risk and allows its shareholders to deploy capital to other opportunities.

The deal, naturally, marks the end of my coverage of Avadel with a relatively modest 35% return since I initiated coverage in January 2024, and a very decent 134% gain since I reiterated my bullish rating in January 2025.

The settlement is also very relevant for Jazz, on which I continue to have a bullish rating, but overall, I see the deal as more favorable to Avadel (and soon Alkermes), as Jazz only gets modest royalties on Lumryz net sales and a short postponement of a commercial launch of Lumryz in idiopathic hypersomnia. However, that is still more than I assumed for Jazz, and that was little to nothing going its way, and considered any legal wins as upside to the investment thesis.

Settlement is a clear win for Avadel, although both were hoping for a lot more

Let us compare what each company is getting.

Avadel is getting:

  • $90 million in cash.
  • Jazz will waive its right to receive royalties or damages on past sales of Lumryz through September 30, 2025.
  • A perpetual, worldwide license, that allows Avadel to freely operate with Lumryz globally without caveats.
  • Jazz has agreed not to challenge the approvability of Lumryz in any indication.
  • Jazz has agreed that Avadel can sell Lumryz for the treatment of idiopathic hypersomnia after March 1, 2028.

Jazz's side of the deal:

  • Avadel will not challenge Jazz’s Xywav and Xyrem patents, related to any indication, strength, conditions of use, dosage forms, and presentations.
  • Starting in October, Avadel will pay royalties of 3.85% (subject to a potential reduction to 3.75%) of net sales of Lumryz in narcolepsy through February 2036.
  • The above-mentioned launch of Lumryz in idiopathic hypersomnia cannot occur prior to March 1, 2028, and this amounts to a potential delay of a year, or less (assuming positive phase 3 data next year and what would have been FDA approval in 2027).
  • Starting on March 1, 2028, Jazz will receive royalties of 10% (subject to a potential reduction to 9.5%) on Lumryz for any future indication until February 2036.

Summing it up, Avadel gets cash from Jazz, and freedom to operate in exchange for modest royalties on Lumryz's sales through February 2036.

I think Avadel is the clear winner here, with the caveat that Avadel bulls were expecting substantial damages from the lawsuit against Jazz. For example, in its open letter to Avadel shareholders, the ASL Strategic Value Fund quoted Avadel’s management saying that the damages payment could exceed $1 billion, and they ended up settling for $90 million. Of course, this is a broader deal than just the mentioned damages and the freedom to operate is worth far more than the damages payment.

But Jazz should not be too unhappy with this deal. The settlement eliminates the mentioned billion-plus damages payment risk, and they were unlikely to prevent Avadel from selling Lumryz. As as such, getting the relatively modest royalties on Lumryz sales can be considered a small win as well.

Alkermes is getting a bargain

Alkermes will pay approximately $18.50 per share in cash for Avadel, or approximately $1.95 billion, and another $1.50 per share, or approximately $150 million, if Lumryz is approved for the treatment of idiopathic hypersomnia before the end of 2028.

Considering the settlement with Jazz that eliminates all legal overhangs on Lumryz, this looks like a disappointing deal for Avadel. Yes, the launch of Lumryz has underperformed expectations (including my own) after a strong start, but its momentum has improved lately.

With the current growth trajectory in narcolepsy and, as of today, a very likely approval for the treatment of idiopathic hypersomnia, I believe Lumryz was on track to become a $1 billion+ drug a year for Avadel by the early 2030s.

And there is a lot of operating leverage potential here since the sales force is established and the second indication would require very little in terms of additional spending. This combination of sales and profits would result in a net present value of $33-35 per share in my model, and fitting the valuation to the $18.50 to $20.00 per share range results in $685-740 million in net sales in 2031.

And Lumryz within Alkermes is probably worth even more if we take into account the SG&A leverage of a multiproduct company versus a single product company that is Avadel.

Avadel’s management and the board determined that staying independent was not in the best interests of the shareholders, and while I am disappointed with the exit value, I can see why they believe that is the case. With the legal risks from Jazz lawsuits in the rearview mirror, these are the risks a standalone Avadel would take (and which Alkermes will inherit):

  • Increased competition from the freely available generic Xyrem. As a reminder, Jazz had settlements with generic filers that allowed them to sell limited quantities of the authorized generic version through the end of 2025. As of January 2026, generic versions of Xyrem will flood the U.S. market and Avadel apparently did not want to take that risk.
  • Continued fierce competition from Jazz. Lumryz did not take as much market share as I expected it would considering its improved convenience (once nightly dosing versus two times for Jazz’s Xyrem and Xywav).
  • Competitive threats from Alkermes’ itself and other companies developing orexin 2 receptor agonists. Alkermes has recently presented very promising results of alixorexton in narcolepsy patients, and if approved, these candidates could take significant market share, and negatively impact the growth trajectory of Lumryz.

I have no rating on Alkermes, but believe that, all things considered, they are getting a good deal:

  • Avadel was cash flow positive and profitable in Q2, and the deal will be immediately accretive to its earnings.
  • Lumryz diversifies its revenue base, and Alkermes will have a sales force ready to promote its orexin 2 receptor agonist or agonists (since it has three in development) for narcolepsy, idiopathic hypersomnia, and potentially other sleep disorders.

Conclusion

The settlement with Jazz cleared the path for Avadel to be acquired by Alkermes. I believe Alkermes is getting a good deal considering the removal of the legal overhang through a favorable settlement, and the growth prospects of Lumryz in narcolepsy and idiopathic hypersomnia.

Jazz also got something from the settlement as it delayed the market arrival of Lumryz in idiopathic hypersomnia to March 2028 and as it will get some upside from Lumryz royalties.

data.title: Kimberly-Clark: The Day Of Reckoning Is Nigh
data.description:
Kimberly-Clark Corporation's (NYSE:KMB) stock is on a path to becoming an utter shareholder disaster. The main reason why most market participants are missing the forest for the trees is the extreme short-termism within the equity market.

Most analysts and investors are focused on the quarterly results, the latest restructuring program, or the notion that a current dividend yield of above 4% is somehow a very attractive feature.

Before we zoom out and take a closer look at the big picture for KMB, we should mention that the stock has performed relatively well over the past 3-year period.

Since I first initiated my coverage on KMB, with a "Sell" rating back in mid-2020, the stock has delivered a total return of almost 2%, while the S&P 500 has more than doubled.

Past performance is hardly a reason to avoid a stock and could often lead to very attractive and massively undervalued opportunities. In the case of KMB, however, this is only an illustration of what could be expected during good periods for equities overall.

To fully understand what the main problems of KMB stock are, we should zoom out by at least 20 years and take a closer look at what has happened within the company during this long time frame. Doing so would also allow us to see where the company is headed and why shareholders might soon face the harsh reality of investing in a business that is failing to adapt.

The Big Picture

As a starting point, Kimberly-Clark's free cash flow has not changed by much over the past 20 years. At the very best, one could make an argument that it has gone up from an average of $1.5 billion during the 2005-2008 period to roughly $2.5 billion in FY 2024. Even if we use that logic, however, this equates to an annual increase of the free cash flow at around 3%, which is no longer enough to cover the annual rate of inflation.

This would not be a problem for shareholders if KMB management had been busy transforming the business through strategic M&A activities and by supplementing this strategy with more growth-oriented capex in key segments of the market.

Instead, over the reviewed period, KMB has been doing something completely different. The free cash flow has been used in two major areas. First, there have been share buybacks, which have been necessary to reduce total shares outstanding and create the illusion of growth.

Second, there has been the need to gradually increase the dividend payouts as the dividend yield needs to be kept competitive against other large consumer staple businesses. As a result of that, however, the share of annual dividends to the company's cash flow from operations has increased from 28% in 2004 to almost 60% as of today.

This results in less and less cash flow available to achieve strategic objectives. Moreover, it is an unsustainable trend that will have to come to an end soon.

The reason why I say that is because as annual increases of cash flow from operations failed to keep up with the dividend and share buybacks, KMB is now in a situation that it would either need to divest more parts of its business or increase its leverage to cover the annual cash outflows.

The higher cash flow from operations in 2023 and 2024 (see the graph above) and the divestment of its international tissue business has allowed the management to reduce its net debt in recent years. However, as we could see on the graph below, we are already seeing a slight increase in recent months, and it appears highly likely that the net debt will continue to increase in the coming years.

To make matters worse, the interest rates on long-term debt have been rising, and this will only put more pressure on KMB management to alter its capital allocation policies.

Finally, the level of capital expenditures has been insufficient to cover the annual depreciation expense in recent years. The ratio between the two has fallen below 100%, which is the ultimate minimum to support ongoing operations. A ratio of above 100% would be needed for the company to return to organic growth mode, without relying solely on price increases.

After everything said so far, it appears that KMB is between a rock and a hard place. The business is struggling to grow, and on one hand it would need more cash to return to growth - either organically or through M&A, but on the other hand, the level of shareholder distributions has reached levels that do not allow for this to happen.

As a result, for long-term shareholders it is largely irrelevant what happens during quarterly results, but what truly matters is where the current management is steering the business from a capital allocation point of view. After following the company for more than 5 years now, I see no material change in that regard, which leads me to believe that even the best-case scenarios for KMB shareholders are not very appealing.

The Best Case Scenarios

Perhaps the best-case scenario is the only way out; if KMB is acquired or it merges with another large entity. With a market cap of around $40 billion, such a transaction appears less likely, and it will be a very large deal for almost all consumer staple businesses.

The question, however, remains about who will be interested in such a transaction. Procter & Gamble (PG) is the major peer of KMB, and to me it appears highly unlikely that they will be interested in acquiring even more brands in the tissue and feminine segments.

Unilever (UL) would be the second choice that comes to mind, but the company has already faced shareholder backlash for its intention to acquire GlaxoSmithKline's (GSK) consumer healthcare business in 2022. Moreover, the company is also moving in a direction towards higher-margin products in the Beauty and Wellbeing segments. For these reasons, I would be highly surprised if UL management expressed interest in KMB.

The U.K. giant Reckitt Benckiser Group Plc (OTCPK:RBGPF) could be a potential suitor given the company's focus on health and hygiene consumer brands. The company, however, has been undergoing significant restructuring in recent years and has just recently announced the divestment of its Essential Home business. With that move, it seems that Reckitt's management is now more focused on higher-margin health businesses as opposed to expanding the portfolio of hygiene related brands.

The Essential Home portfolio includes the global brands Air Wick, Calgon, Woolite, Cillit Bang, Resolve, Sole and Easy-Off, as well as around 75 other brands across over 70 markets.

Source: Reckitt website

Colgate-Palmolive (CL) is the next big enterprise in the sector, but similarly to its peers, the company is oriented towards high-margin product segments, such as skin care and pet food.

Next in line would be the German giant Henkel (OTCPK:HENKY), but the company has recently divested some non-core businesses in North America, which would not tie in with a strategy to acquire a company like KMB.

This leaves smaller peers for a potential merger. Companies like Clorox (CLX) or even Church & Dwight (CHD) could be potential fit, albeit such a transaction is highly speculative and unlikely to deliver shareholder value.

Lastly, there are the private equity investors or a potential activist investor to completely restructure the business. These, in my view, are the most likely scenarios, but the outcome for KMB shareholders is still highly uncertain.

Until then, I would expect KMB to continue muddling through with more divestments being announced and leverage continuing to increase. None of that is appealing for long-term investors and, in my view, cannot justify either a "Buy" or "Hold" rating.

Conclusion

While many investors remain focused on short-term outcomes for KMB, the sad reality is that the company is now between a rock and a hard place. The trend in capital allocation that has been going on for decades is slowly coming to an end, and this could end up in a disaster for shareholders. The only way out of this situation appears to be KMB becoming a target for a larger entity, private equity, or an activist investor. This, however, is largely speculative, and in my view, not enough of a reason to justify either a "Buy" or "Hold" rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pyrostone Holdings Aims For $6 Million U.S. IPO
data.description:
Pyrostone Is Growing, But Valuation Is Excessive

Pyrostone Holdings Ltd. (PYST) has filed to raise $6 million in an IPO of its Class A ordinary shares, according to SEC F-1 registration information.

PYST provides business valuation and advisory services in Hong Kong.

The company is growing revenue profitably from a small base, but its valuation expectations are excessive and the firm is thinly capitalized and faces many risks.

My outlook on the PYST IPO is to sell (avoid it).

What Does Pyrostone Do?

Hong Kong, China-based Pyrostone Holdings Limited was founded to develop competencies in various valuation markets and provide more general business advisory services.

Management is led by Chairman, CEO and Director Mr. Chun Fai, NG, who has been with the firm since 2019 and was previously director of Access Partner Consultancy & Appraisals Limited and worked for PricewaterhouseCoopers Hong Kong.

The company's primary offerings include:

Valuation Services

  • Real Estate.

  • Business.

  • Financial Instruments.

  • Long Service Payments.

Business Advisory Services

  • ESG.

  • Internal Control Review.

  • Market Research.

  • Secondary Reviews.

The pie chart below shows the revenue contribution percentage for its two practice areas:

Pyrostone has booked fair market value investment of only $2,800 as of March 31, 2025 from investors.

Most of the investment in the company has been through bank borrowings, which totaled approximately $413,000 as of March 31, 2025.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenues have increased, as the figures below indicate:

The General & Administrative efficiency multiple, i.e., how many dollars of additional revenue are produced from each dollar of General & Administrative expense, was 0.5x in the most recent reporting period. (Source - SEC.)

What Is Pyrostone’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by Mordor Intelligence, the Hong Kong market for management consulting services is an estimated $0.9 billion in 2025 and is projected to grow to $1.31 billion by the end of 2030.

This represents a projected CAGR of 5.98% from 2025 to 2030.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are the city's growing role as a financial hub and a desire by companies to continually improve their operating efficiencies.

Also, the valuation services market is likely smaller than the general management consulting industry, but probably has similar growth characteristics in the medium term.

The firm faces intense competition from a wide variety of market participants. The market has relatively low barriers to entry and is highly fragmented.

The company seeks new clients through its direct marketing efforts and through referrals.

Its customers include Hong Kong Stock Exchange-listed companies in the industries of education, finance, pharmaceuticals, natural resources, food and beverage, construction, technology and automotive sectors.

Pyrostone’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

Pyrostone’s IPO Plan

The float-to-outstanding-shares ratio (excluding underwriter options) will be approximately 5.08%, which means the stock will be a very "low float" stock subject to high volatility in open market trading.

According to Pyrostone’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

Approximately 60% for expanding operating scale and hiring additional staff.

Approximately 20% for enhancing our brand.

Approximately 20% for working capital and other general corporate purposes.

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is not available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, management said that the company has not been involved in any litigation or administrative actions that would have a material adverse effect on its business or financial condition.

Pyrostone is seeking U.S. public capital market investment for its general corporate purposes.

The company’s financials have generated increasing topline revenue, growing gross profit and gross margin, higher operating profit but lower cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended March 31, 2025, was $416,300.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have dropped as revenue has grown and its General & Administrative efficiency multiple was 0.5x in the most recent fiscal year.

The firm currently plans to pay zero dividends and will keep any future earnings and available funds for its development and working capital needs.

PYST’s recent capital spending history shows it hasn’t spent on capital expenditures in the last fiscal year.

The firm’s growth strategy includes growing its professional team, expanding its service offerings and promoting its brand in the various service markets it operates in.

The market opportunity for providing valuation and management consulting services in Hong Kong is moderately robust but features intense competition from participants of various sizes and an expectation of only moderate growth in the coming five-year period.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its "emerging growth company" and "foreign private issuer" status, which will allow management to produce less information for shareholders.

Many such company stocks have produced subpar returns for shareholders post-IPO.

PYST has geographic revenue concentration risks, with all of its revenue coming from clients located in the Hong Kong area.

The firm has various related party transactions and relationships, as detailed here.

As to valuation, Pyrostone is proposed to value itself at an EV/EBITDA multiple of around 82x, an excessive valuation.

Given ultra-high valuation assumptions, thin capitalization, and many risks, my outlook on the IPO is to sell (avoid it).
