data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: The Bidders Perspectives
data.description:
The Bidding Game

An interesting game is unfolding in front of industry observers and investors as several huge and globally influential companies are circling Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD), and each for their own reasons. Indeed, it is not simply a bunch of predators trying to get as much prey for the smallest possible price – some of the fish are probably not even interested in hunting. And as far as the prey itself is concerned, there are concerns it is pricing itself too high and will end up as it had been until a short time ago: a "laughing stock" that makes its investors cry.

WBD: Finally in the Spotlight

As multiple interested parties are approaching Warner Bros. Discovery, among which are apparently Comcast (CMCSA) and Netflix (NFLX), David Zaslav's company finally has its moment:

  1. WBD is continuing to advance the Company's planned separation, thus making a merger easier for those suitors that are interested only in the streaming and studios part of the business.
  2. WBD has officially initiated a comprehensive review of strategic alternatives, i.e. put out the official "for sale" sign.
  3. Finally, "the Company will also consider an alternative separation structure that would enable a merger of Warner Bros. and spin-off of Discovery Global to our shareholders".

As somebody leaked the apparent minimum price tag of $30, the stock has already moved a lot. The higher it goes, the easier it will be for Mr. Zaslav to raise the price tag. However, despite having apparently rejected a $23-$24 bid by Paramount Skydance (PSKY), WBD stock still hovers around the $20 mark. Quite evidently, investors are skeptical, even though Warner Bros. Discovery is officially doing everything it can to enable a deal.

Investors can start dreaming about making north of 50% in very little time – or being afraid of everything falling apart, with the stock tumbling back to the low to mid teens.

The current $20 market price reflects the perceived equally high probability of a 25% gain and a 25% loss. Or a 50% gain/50% loss, which are both seen as less likely. So $20 represents the current equilibrium.

Which is kind of odd, considering that a bidding war (that apparently is already ongoing) is the best case scenario for WBD – and the bidders involved certainly have the means to correctly evaluate the business and pay a fair price. If investors had some sort of certainty that the bidding will end up in any kind of deal, the current market price should be higher.

Apple: Always in the Room

Apple (AAPL), which always seems to get thrown into the mix as a potential suitor, remains just as silent as usual on the subject. Personally I don't believe it is really interested in taking over WBD's streaming assets. Apple certainly would like to own the studios and HBO (it has previously expressed interest in owning it), but would probably have a lot of political issues with many other assets, that are just too far away from Apple's ambition to be a feel-good destination for everybody. At the same time, given the interest from multiple parties, the price is unlikely to be low enough to justify a purchase followed by a massive clean-up of the library.

That said, Apple could still throw itself into the mix to achieve completely different goals: For example, it might simply want to drive up the price tag, in order to weaken the competitor which ultimately wins the bidding war. It might also want to signal interest in specific parts of Warner Bros. Discovery, in order to blur the picture, delay any potential deal, and, maybe, crash and ruin David Zaslav's party once and for all.

Amazon and Paramount Skydance: The Rich Opportunists

Both Amazon (AMZN) and Paramount Skydance already have their own studios. Why would they need another studio? – Well, so that nobody else gets it. Plus, they would enlarge their content libraries and subscriber counts, while enormously increasing their services' appeal in international markets.

However, considering that they would not really need those studios, it is unlikely that they will pay the highest price.

Comcast: Trying to Stay Relevant

In contrast, Comcast, being one of those smaller Netflix competitors that badly needs greater scale, should be one of those companies willing to pay a high price for WBD's studio and streaming units. On the other hand, any large-scale merger needs government approval, and Brian Roberts doesn't enjoy Donald Trump's favor. The president has not shied away from direct interference with industrial mega-mergers. Yet even David Ellison somehow managed to get his merger across the line after having outright donated to Joe Biden's election campaign. So don't count Comcast out. In my view, it remains one of the most likely winners of the bidding war – for the simple reason that it probably has the most to lose if one of its competitors wins the prize.

Netflix: Can Everything, Must Nothing

In the context of movie streaming, Netflix is the only company that has done what all its competitors are still trying to achieve. And it managed to do it without owning any major studios. Yet there could still be good reasons for Netflix to enter the fray as well.

In contrast to all other interested parties in the bidding war, Netflix has already commented on the topic during its Q3 earnings call. The lengthy commentary was clearly well prepared and sounds kind of specular compared to WBD's initially mentioned press release:

  1. Netflix doesn't need to buy assets, it has demonstrated that it can build things on its own and is self-sufficient. It is not afraid of greater competition, if any competitor ends up merging with WBD.
  2. In the interest of its shareholders, Netflix has an obligation to look at any opportunity and evaluate it.
  3. Netflix evaluates any capital allocation decision within the following framework:

If it's IP, does it strengthen our entertainment offering? Is there additional value in ownership? Does it strengthen our existing capabilities somehow? Does it accelerate our existing strategy? And by the way, and you look at all these things relative to the price relative to the opportunity cost and relative to other alternatives.

The last few lines basically clarify why Netflix is very interested in WBD and would provide the perfect rationale for buying it. WBD has tons of great IP, owning the studios would enormously enhance Netflix's capabilities, and owning it would accelerate growth for the market leader.

That said, I would expect Netflix to be interested, but probably not at the highest price.

Finally, Reed Hastings is known to be a Democratic mega-donor. The fact that a WBD-NFLX merger would make the combined company even more unassailable by its competitors than Netflix already is, would provide easy arguments for the Trump administration to deny its authorization.

WBD investors: What if Zaslav Screws Up?

The story for WBD investors has always been the same. Fundamentally speaking, its key assets were unattractive on their own, but a lot more interesting for different owners – the question was whether anybody would make an offer and whether the offer would be high enough – especially considering that everybody knew that a standalone WBD would remain a melting ice cube.

Waiting was the name of the game. For WBD it meant to be stubborn, optimize its balance sheet, prepare the company for a potential split and (maybe only partial) merger, while avoiding any obvious signs of weakness. For its suitors it meant to wait for just that: a moment of weakness to exploit. And the longer the suitors waited, the cheaper their prey would likely become.

As David Ellison tried to take his competitors by surprise and made his offer before the split, other parties were forced to enter the game earlier than expected.

There apparently have been at least two offers, which were both deemed unattractive. The one by a yet unidentified bidder was higher that Paramount's but probably also below $30/share.

So finally somebody is willing to offer decent money for WBD's assets, although still much less than once hoped. One or two years from now the price probably would not have been higher – quite the contrary. This means time is of the essence.

If WBD investors knew for sure David Zaslav will make a deal, any deal, even if it is below the $30 mark, the stock would be a screaming buy right now. But most WBD investors I know don't put a lot of faith into Mr. Zaslav's capabilities. They've "lost their religion" long ago and know all this might end up like the homonymous R.E.M. song:

Try, cry, why cry?

That was just a dream, just a dream

Just a dream, dream

data.title: Betting On The AI Backbone Of The West: The Bullish Case For Palantir Technologies
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) is a premier AI software company that is uniquely positioned to capture much of the downstream economic value of the AI revolution. Despite its high premium, this factor still makes me bullish on the outlook of the stock. More specifically, the company is transitioning from a government contractor to a hyper-growing commercial software leader.

Palantir Overview

Palantir is a leading provider of proprietary software platforms that are used for data-driven decision-making in highly complex environments. In particular, the company works closely with Western-allied nations.

The company’s government segment specializes in working with defense and intelligence communities that primarily use Palantir Gotham. As a result, the company is often one of the first contacted by allied governments when a high-stakes conflict develops. These contracts provide the company with a stable source of income, but they do introduce complexity and political risk.

While government contracts offer Palantir stability, its rapidly emerging commercial segment is one of the primary strengths that I see for the company moving forward. A key element of this segment’s success is the Artificial Intelligence Platform. This platform was originally targeted towards very large organizations, and it is designed to help non-technical users improve productivity through leveraging large language models. This platform makes it easier for AI models to orchestrate crucial enterprise needs while maintaining context.

Catalysts For Palantir

Revenue Milestone

The company saw a record second quarter of 2025, achieving $1 billion in quarterly revenue. This was a first for the company to hit this impressive goal. This represents 48% year-over-year growth for the company. The primary driver of this growth was the U.S. commercial segment as its Artificial Intelligence Platform obtained much wider adoption. In fact, this segment’s revenue expanded by 93% year-over-year.

Rapid Deal Conversion

I see Palantir’s new sales approach as being a key component in the success of its growth in the commercial segment. The company’s “boot camp” approach to closing deals with customers allows it to quickly and efficiently close deals with new clients. As a result, the company has an easier time with consistent quarter-over-quarter growth, which has led to it gaining hundreds of customers.

Winning Major Contracts

Palantir has had ample success recently with securing major government contracts. I find the $10 billion deal with the U.S. Army to be particularly notable. In addition to being a large deal, it is also spread out over ten years. This provides it with a major revenue stream that it can use to continue to expand. The company also closed a major deal with the U.K. government, helping it expand into health and energy.

Palantir's Financial Overview

Palantir Technologies shows a business undergoing major financial transformations. It is rapidly evolving from a high-growth but unprofitable enterprise into one that is showing increasing financial discipline and success.

Revenue Trajectory and Growth Engine

Strong revenue underscores Palantir’s position as a major player in the rapidly developing analytics and AI markets. More specifically, its successful efforts at scaling its operations are driving substantial top-line expansion.

I see Palantir’s consistent and robust revenue growth as evidence of it solidifying its market penetration. In particular, I am impressed by the company’s Trailing Twelve Months revenue growth, which is currently a robust 38.79%. Its historical revenue trend also helps to reinforce this, as it has increased from $1.54 billion in 2021 to $3.44 billion TTM. This strong performance is also largely expected to continue, with its forward revenue growth estimated at 36.16%.

Palantir’s Turn toward the Black

A key characteristic of Palantir is that it operates with the strong gross margins of a high-value software enterprise. For example, the company’s TTM Gross Profit Margin is an astonishing 80.03%, and I see this as reflecting the company’s inherent value, pricing power, and the lower marginal cost of its software. I see this consistent upward trend in Gross Profit over the last few years as indicating that revenue growth is being efficiently translated into gross earnings. This provides the company with a solid foundation for covering operating expenses and achieving net profitability.

Palantir’s Improving Margins

Despite the company’s past historical operating losses, it has made remarkable strides in addressing this issue. The progression from previous years’ loss to a TTM Operating Income of $569.5 million shows successful expense management. In particular, the company’s focus on efficiency has flowed directly to its bottom line. This is evidenced by the TTM Net Income Margin of 22.18%. Additionally, I see the recent positive Net Income of $763.3 million. This is a critical milestone that signals a sustained and positive shift in its financial health.

Balance Sheet Strength and Stability

Another one of Palantir’s strengths that I see is its strong liquidity position. In particular, this is reinforced by its strong cash and short-term investments that total $6.0 billion. This large cash cushion helps to signal outstanding short-term financial health. As a result, the company could cover its short-term liabilities multiple times over with its liquid assets.

In addition to the company’s ample cash cushion, it also has a minimal debt burden. For example, I find its current total debt of $237.8 million to be extremely low when compared to its multi-billion-dollar cash cushion and growing revenue. This results in the company having a negative net debt, where the cash holdings far exceed the total debt of the company. This negative net debt is a positive and illustrates a conservative and financially sound approach to capital management.

Palantir Stock Valuation

The Valuation Dilemma

Despite the stock’s price of approximately $181, Palantir’s valuation is still warranted by its superior, long-term AI-driven growth prospects. Additionally, it has a market capitalization of a staggering $422.63 billion, with an enterprise value that closely follows at $416.97 billion. I see these figures as showcasing the market’s high expectations for the company’s future growth and profitability. Considering its positioning in the rapidly growing AI ecosystem, these expectations seem fairly reasonable.

Palantir’s Revenue Multiples vs. Peers

While Palantir has a revenue multiple that is at an extreme value, its TTM price-to-sales ratio sits at 119.99, and its TTM EV/sales ratio is similar at 121.19. This is far higher than established enterprise software peers like SAP (SAP) and Salesforce (CRM). This helps to indicate the market’s confidence in the company’s superior future growth potential and trajectory in the critical AI platform segment.

Palantir’s earnings ratios are further evidence of the forward-looking nature of the stock. While its TTM P/E based on GAAP earnings is an extremely high 590.52, the forward P/E based on Non-GAAP earnings is more palatable but still high at 277.28. This leads me to conclude that investors are paying a premium for the velocity of growth rather than historical profit.

Investors are paying a premium for the velocity of growth rather than the static historical profit. Reflecting its asset-light model, it showcases the strength of its intellectual capital.

Earnings Estimates

An indicator that reinforces my bullish outlook is the growth in expected EPS. These forecasts expect it to grow from $0.64 in December 2025 to $0.85 in December 2026. The high number of recent upward EPS revisions is another bullish indicator that shows me the company is continuing to develop a lucrative business model.

Palantir’s bull case largely rests on its true potential as the foundational operational system for Western AI. Palantir’s AI Platform offers it a structural advantage that makes a strong case for its price tag. More specifically, I see a company that has repeatedly exceeded expectations, and it consistently beats earnings estimates, which adds to the argument that management can execute its vision. The combination of FCF margins, accelerating commercial adoption, and clear pathways to scaling suggests that the market may have only begun to price in the company’s potential for dominance in the critical AI sector.

Projecting the Future Price of Palantir

To calculate a potential future price for the stock, I apply a forward P/E multiple of 265x to the conservative consensus for 2026 EPS of $0.85, which yields a potential target price of $225. Despite this multiple being extremely high, it is still slightly below the current forward P/E, and it reflects the high-growth profile and market dominance Palantir is expected to achieve. Furthermore, the potential for accelerating growth in its commercial segment and its long-term government contracts makes this target reasonable.

Risks to Palantir

Failing to Sustain Hyper-Growth

In order to justify a bullish outlook with the high multiples, requires Palantir to sustain its impressive growth. If these rates slow substantially, the stock’s future could be less promising, as it is currently pricing in many years of sustainable growth. This exposes the company to an elevated risk, as even disappointing but still positive results could cause outsized volatility.

Loss of Its Competitive Edge

A key strength that I see for Palantir is its first-mover advantage. Furthermore, the advanced capabilities of its systems offer a competitive moat that most companies will simply be unable to catch up to. However, the risk that major players, such as Google (GOOG) and Microsoft (MSFT), can potentially develop software rivaling Palantir’s capabilities. If this were to occur, it could pose a major threat to the company’s future prospects.

Failing to Penetrate Global Commercial Markets

I find Palantir’s commercial growth in the U.S. to be highly impressive. However, if the company is to sustain the growth, it needs to justify its premium; it will have to successfully expand in the global commercial market. Failing in this goal could cause Palantir to effectively be capped. If the company fails, it could create openings for rival firms to capture market share.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I find Palantir to be promising but speculative. With its expansion into commercial segments, it will potentially unlock the sustainable growth that is needed to justify its current premium. Its Artificial Intelligence Platform is a strong selling point for commercial clients, and its current “boot camp” approach to selling quickly incorporates new commercial clients into its ecosystem. Despite the potential headwinds the company faces, its impressive growth and future prospects make me confident that it will be able to continue growing, which is why I have estimated a potential price target of $225.

data.title: Navitas: Fundamentals Are Bearish But You Can't Fight Animal Spirits
data.description:
Performance assessment

I very rarely short, but I made an exception for Navitas Semiconductor (NASDAQ:NVTS). I think out of my coverage in Seeking Alpha, I have only had one other short. That was on Cronos Group (CRON) (CRON:CA) and it played out very well. But the market has really punished me for this one on Navitas:

Thesis

You know, I am still fundamentally bearish on Navitas. But the market has reminded me of a lesson I already knew but did not respect:

The market does not move solely on fundamentals.

So I felt forced to throw in the towel change my positioning even if I retain my bearish skepticism:

  1. The bullish story is based on hopes of becoming a key supplier to Nvidia
  2. The near term revenue outlook is very challenged
  3. A foundry vendor transition brings about a major execution risk
  4. The company is well-funded to sustain current levels of cash bleed for 3 years
  5. Valuations show clear signs of overhype
  6. NVTS technicals are clearly bullish as large advances upward are supported by high volumes

If you are unfamiliar with Navitas Semiconductor, I highly recommend reading my initiating coverage on the stock. There, I have provided a business brief and explained what various jargon such as GaN and SiC mean.

The bullish story is based on hopes of becoming a key supplier to Nvidia

As I mentioned in my initiating coverage of Navitas, the market seems to be very gung-ho on the company simply because it is collaborating with Nvidia (NVDA) on next-generation high-voltage DC (direct current, which uses reduces energy loss compared to alternating current aka AC) power architecture for use in its AI factory data centers. My concern at that time was that this was merely a development collaboration without any tangible signs of commercial impacts just yet. And that remains the case even today. However, there is a key positive on the development side:

The company recently made progress in its development of medium and high voltage 800 volts of direct current [VDC] GaN and SiC power chips. Navitas is supposedly developing products that are custom-built for Nvidia's AI factories in a way that results in:

breakthrough efficiency, power density, and performance

- Seeking Alpha News

Again; this was just a development update. We still don't know what the commercial implications for Navitas would be. In any case, that is at least 2 years away as volume production is expected in 2027:

Development timeline: For each stage, initial customer evaluations are complete with final engineering samples expected in Q4; anticipate final supplier selections and system designs completed in 2026 in advance of volume production in 2027.

- Navitas Q2 FY25 Press Release

Yet, the stock jumped almost 72% after this development update news, adding $1.5 billion in market cap:

This looks like hype to me. There seems to a be a lot of hope driving this stock that is arguably unwarranted as Navitas itself has disclosed that nothing commercially is really contractually confirmed:

our collaboration with Nvidia does not involve any binding commitments by Nvidia or any customer, and there is no guarantee that we will achieve any revenues as a result

- Q2 FY25 10Q

Also note that Navitas has competition from larger ($22 billion market cap) players such as ON Semiconductor Corporation (ON) in VDCs too. It's not like its got any special, exclusive relationship with Nvidia on these development collaborations.

The near term revenue outlook is very challenged

Navitas' chips are used to handle the flow of power in electronic systems. This power segment of the semiconductor industry is in a downturn:

Our industry is in a classic semiconductor downturn with a slowdown in projections for solar, industrial, EV sectors, the continued impact of tariff conflicts, and now, the removal of tax credits for the solar and EV industry.

- Previous CEO Eugene Sheridan in the Q2 FY25 earnings call

This explains part of the decreasing revenue trend over recent quarters:

Moreover, the company has material exposure to China revenues, as they make up more than 60% of the overall revenue mix:

So management has cut their revenue guidance due to tariff impacts and decisions to scale out of some Chinese mobile business where Navitas' chips are used in applications such as fast-charging:

Moving on to guidance for the third quarter, we currently expect revenue of $10 million plus or minus $500,000. This expected revenue reduction reflects both adverse impacts from China tariff risks for our silicon carbide business and our strategic decision to de-prioritize lower margin China mobile business while we accelerate our investment and leadership in AI data centers and associated new energy infrastructure.

- CFO Todd Glickman in the Q2 FY25 earnings call

Overall, this led to a big 35% revenue guidance miss for the Q3 FY25 outlook:

So given that the revenues from any potential collaboration with Nvidia are coming in only in 2027 and the business is facing a combination of a downturn and tariff-related hurdles, I think it is fair to say the revenue outlook over the next year is challenged, to say the least.

A foundry vendor transition brings about a major execution risk

To add to its woes, Navitas has a highly fragile business model due to high customer concentration (the top customer makes up 54% of overall revenues) and supplier concentration too. Now, the supplier concentration risk is particularly relevant because the company used to rely solely on TSMC (TSM) (OTC:TSMWF) to produce GAN chip wafers. In the company's 10-Q, it had noted that the loss of the relationship with this foundry supplier could have a "substantial negative effect" on Navitas. And unfortunately, that scenario is playing out now, and the company is left scrambling to arrange a supply of GaN chip wafers from Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation:

On July 1, 2025, TSMC announced its intention to cease GaN production in July 2027. While we have taken proactive steps to mitigate this risk, including expanding our collaboration with Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing Corporation (“Powerchip”), with qualification of initial devices expected in the fourth quarter of 2025 and mass production anticipated in the first half of 2026, there is no assurance that Powerchip or any other supplier will meet our quality, volume, cost, or timeline requirements... Any delays or disruptions in qualifying or ramping production with Powerchip or other alternative suppliers could negatively impact our ability to fulfill customer orders, lead to increased costs, or cause a loss of revenue and market share. In addition, the transition may require us to modify our designs or manufacturing processes, which could result in increased engineering costs and potential delays in product availability. We are continuing to evaluate the impact of this transition on our financial statements, and there can be no assurance that our mitigation efforts will fully address the risks associated with TSMC’s exit from GaN production. Our failure to secure a stable and diversified supply of GaN wafers could materially adversely affect our business, financial condition, and results of operations.

- Q2 FY25 10Q, Author's italicized highlights

By H1 FY26, we will know how successfully the company will have managed this supplier transition. In the upcoming quarters, management's commentary on the rolling status of this would be a key monitorable.

The company is well-funded to sustain current levels of cash bleed for 3 years

Navitas is burning cash at a rough rate of $13 million per quarter:

As with any cash-burning company, I always look at how much funding runway there is until the company runs out of money. And in this case, Navitas is arguably comfortably positioned. Thanks to a recent $100 million equity raise, its liquidity coffers have been boosted to $161 million:

This gives a 161/13 = a bit more than 12 quarters or 3 years of funding runway left.

Valuations show clear signs of overhype

When I last covered NVTS, the stock was trading at an almost 5x higher 1-yr fwd EV/Revenue premium vs the median of its comps. I thought that was frothy. But now, NVTS stock is at an incredible 85.5x 1-yr fwd revenue multiple; this is more than 20x the comps' median multiple of 4.3x:

I say this is insane. Also note how despite the earnings expectations index going down, the valuation multiple has just exploded upward:

This kind of pattern is representative of genuine, unsustainable hype in my opinion. So I think bulls should still express extreme caution when it comes to their exposure to Navitas.

NVTS technicals are clearly bullish as large advances upward are supported by high volumes

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of NVTS vs SPX500

Although Navitas' fundamentals look bearish. My view on NVTS stock technicals is quite the opposite. On the monthly ratio charts of NVTS vs SPX500, we see swift progress upward. The price action is clearly bullish. What I had missed considering earlier was the fact that these upward thrusts are happening at high volumes, indicating strong conviction by the bulls. I see no reason to fight this trend and remain bearish on the stock.

Takeaway & Positioning

I retain my bearish fundamental outlook on Navitas as I think the bullish hopes and expectations on eventually becoming a key supplier to Nvidia is extreme. This is especially because the near term revenue outlook is weak due to high China sales exposure and a downturn in this niche power segment of the overall semiconductor market. The company is bleeding cash too, although this is not a big concern yet as there is enough funding runway to last 3 years.

I believe the valuations on NVTS stock are extreme and clearly show irrational exuberance. Yet, the stock technicals are convincingly bullish as the advances up are supported by high volumes.

This is definitely a weird one. Overall, I rate the company a 'Market Perform/Hold' to balance out my bearish fundamental view with the very bullish technical action. I think this is a stock to just avoid.

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: Applied Digital: Powering The AI Boom
data.description:
The Backbone of AI Infrastructure

Applied Digital Corporation (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:APLD) has delivered a set of results that have not gone unnoticed, and after the rally the stock has experienced, I believe it’s worth shedding some light on its business model, especially if you’re still trying to map out where it fits within the broader AI infrastructure landscape.

The company’s model revolves around building and managing the physical infrastructure used by firms specializing in high-performance computing (HPC). These clients deploy their own GPUs within Applied Digital’s facilities to provide GPU cloud and HPC services to third parties.

Currently, the company operates two campuses in North Dakota, Jamestown and Ellendale, with 100 MW and 180 MW of capacity, respectively, and another in Garden City, Texas, with 200 MW. Virtually all this capacity is already contracted, and the company plans to expand its installed capacity to more than 1 GW by 2027 through its new project in Harwood (ND), which will add over 500 MW.

If you’re not familiar with the scale of these figures, it helps to compare: companies like Equinix (EQIX) (though operating under a different business model) manage networks exceeding +2 GW worldwide. In contrast, APLD already concentrates 0.5 GW across just three U.S. sites, and reaching 1 GW by 2027 would position the company directly within the growth engine of AI computing.

And there’s no better proof of that than the CoreWeave case. Applied Digital has signed 400 MW worth of lease agreements with CoreWeave across three phases through 2027. These contracts represent more than $10B in guaranteed payments through 2035, with revenue expected to grow from around $80M in 2026 to more than $600M by 2029, once all capacity is fully operational.

From Blockchain Hosting to High-Performance Computing

After reviewing the company’s quarterly results, APLD reported $64.2M in revenue, up 84% year-over-year. However, these figures require some interpretation, since nearly 41% of that total came from tenant fit-out work performed for CoreWeave.

Specifically, the $26.3M in fit-out revenue represents a one-time item related to the build-out of CoreWeave’s first 100 MW building within the Ellendale (Polaris Forge 1) campus. Once this phase is completed, it will transition into recurring lease revenue within the company’s HPC vertical, but for this quarter, it was recognized as part of the construction process.

The same logic applies to expenses. While the company reported $55.6M in cost of revenue, roughly $25M of that total was directly tied to the fit-out project, offset by the related revenue and leaving an effective gross margin of around 5%.

It’s also worth pausing on the $16.6M in stock-based compensation (SBC). APLD recognized the remaining unvested portion of certain incentive plans in a single quarter, but this did not involve any cash outflow. Essentially, the shift from its former blockchain hosting model to its new HPC infrastructure business led management to establish updated incentive programs tied to upcoming performance milestones.

In my opinion, if both the fit-out effect and the SBC expense are excluded, operating expenses would have remained in line with prior quarters. roughly $12M, indicating that cost discipline remains intact.

After these adjustments, the underlying hosting business shows an operating margin between 10% and 15%, well above the negative GAAP margin reported this quarter. In fact, adjusted EBITDA came in positive at $0.5M, which tells me the core business is beginning to sustain itself even before the lease revenues from the CoreWeave agreement start flowing through. The reported loss per share of –$0.11, therefore, reflects accounting timing rather than operational deterioration.

Bridging the Gap Between Market Price and Contracted Value

With the quarterly results now clarified, we can move on to assigning a valuation for APLD’s stock. At the time of writing, the company has a market capitalization of $9.5B and net debt of around $670M, resulting in an enterprise value of roughly $10.2B. In my view, as discussed earlier, the most logical approach to valuing the company is based on EV per contracted MW, which better reflects the economics of its HPC model.

At present, the market is valuing each operational MW at approximately $21M, based on its current EV divided by installed capacity. Meanwhile, the CoreWeave contracts secure about $28M per MW over the next 15 years, roughly $11B in guaranteed payments. This difference suggests to me that the stock still trades below the value implied by its contracted cash flows, representing a discount to the intrinsic worth of its HPC infrastructure.

That said, it’s also useful to compare APLD using the EV/Sales multiple against peers such as EQIX and Digital Realty (DLR). While these companies operate on a global scale, they share a similar underlying data center business structure, making this a valid framework for projection. Based on current projections, by 2028 APLD is expected to reach 1 GW of installed capacity and around $880 million in annual revenue, implying a forward EV/Sales multiple of about 11.6x.

In other words, APLD’s valuation would compress from its current 34x EV/Sales to roughly 11.6x by FY28 as HPC contract revenues begin to consolidate. In my opinion, this multiple is defensible when compared with the profitability and stability of Equinix and Digital Realty. Both maintain EBITDA margins above 40% with steady cash flows, whereas APLD remains in its buildout phase, with a gross margin around 22% and barely positive EBITDA.

To me, these future cash flows are what the market is already pricing in to justify today’s valuation, making an EV/Sales of roughly 12x by FY28 a reasonable assumption.

Under this scenario, with expected revenue of $880M and a 12x multiple, APLD’s enterprise value would reach approximately $10.5B. Dividing this by 279M shares outstanding yields a price per share slightly above $37.

That would be my base valuation with the data currently available. However, there’s additional upside potential. According to the latest earnings call, APLD has an active pipeline of about 4 GW and is in negotiations with two hyperscalers that could add another 400–500 MW before FY28. If any of these agreements materialize, revenue could exceed $1.2B, making a valuation in the $45–$50 range justifiable.

In my opinion, given the company’s current momentum, a price target between $40 and $45 per share reflects the fundamental value of what APLD could achieve within three years, though at those levels, the margin of safety would narrow. If the rally toward that range is driven by new contracts or major announcements, I would recommend re-evaluating whether it’s worth taking partial profits or maintaining the position for further upside.

What Could Break the Bull Case

While the scenario described earlier represents the ideal outcome, and despite the solidity of APLD’s signed contracts, there are also factors that could justify the current price levels as potentially overextended. For example, with just $114M in cash at the end of the quarter, the company’s liquidity today depends largely on the structured credit facilities provided by Macquarie. If any of these tranches were delayed by more than 90 days or if the construction costs for Polaris Forge exceeded current estimates by 10% or more, operating cash flow could come under pressure, potentially forcing the company to issue new equity to fund ongoing projects.

Ironically, CoreWeave itself represents another risk factor. Assuming the company reaches 1 GW of capacity within the next three years, roughly 80% of total revenue would still be tied to a single customer. Although the contract provides 15 years of visibility and predictable cash flows, any credit-related event or potential price renegotiation could materially impact cash generation.

Finally, one cannot ignore the possibility of a sentiment shift within the broader AI narrative. A slowdown in AI infrastructure demand could trigger multiple compression, with APLD’s EV/Sales contracting from around 12x to 8x, should large-scale projects be postponed or the industry face supply-chain or energy bottlenecks. In that scenario, a combination of liquidity strain, construction delays, or dependency risk on CoreWeave could bring the stock back toward the $20 range, without necessarily invalidating the company’s long-term bullish trend.

A Long-Term Buy on Execution and Scale

With all the data on the table, my reading is that Applied Digital is now at the inflection point where its business model becomes tangible. The CoreWeave agreement not only validates its transition into the HPC infrastructure space but also provides a long-term revenue stream that, if executed according to schedule, could sustain roughly 40% annualized growth over the next three years.

At current levels, I believe the market is already pricing in a significant portion of these future cash flows tied to CoreWeave, as reflected in an implied EV/Sales of around 12x based on FY28 projections. Still, the company’s 4 GW active pipeline and the stability of 15-year take-or-pay contracts make a long-term bullish stance on APLD fundamentally justified.

That said, execution will be key. Any material delay in energizing new capacity or a cooling in AI infrastructure demand could compress the multiple and send the stock temporarily below $20. My base case, however, supports a Buy rating on APLD, with a 36-month price target between $40 and $45 per share.

At those levels, the risk-reward balance would narrow, making it a logical point to consider partial or full profit-taking, unless new contracts or expansion milestones justify revisiting the upside scenario.
